summary: Dan Shapiro comments on Black & Decker's acquisition of Pentair's tool group, which includes respected brands Delta and Porter-Cable. He speculates that the merger will result in a 'slide to the median' where the acquired brands may gain access to cutting-edge innovations but lose some quality as cost-trimming and parts-sharing take effect. He notes that B&D's own quality may improve from the closer association. He concludes there are no short-term impacts on tool buying decisions.
key_points: Black & Decker is acquiring Pentair's tool group including Delta, Porter-Cable, DeVilbiss, and Oldham
Corporate acquisitions tend to produce a 'slide to the median' where premium brands lose quality while budget brands gain it
Porter-Cable and Delta may benefit from B&D's faster adoption of innovations like laser guides
Quality erosion is likely as the acquirer trims costs through parts-sharing and efficiency measures
money_quotes: In my experience, a purchase like this often results in a 'slide to the median'.
Their quality may also sag a bit as they start trying to trim inefficiencies, parts sharing with B&D for example.
The B&D group may similarly improve quality as the groups get closer together.
key_insight: When a mass-market company acquires premium brands, both tend to converge toward mediocrity — the acquired brands lose quality while gaining mainstream innovations.
